translation.en: I like to eat fruits and vegetables.

content.sentence: Tôi thích ăn trái cây ___ rau.

notes_md:
**mà** usually means **“but”** — it connects two ideas that contrast. It’s a simple, very common linker.

It joins two clauses, like English “but,” without changing word order.  

It can also soften a correction or remind someone of something, almost like “but hey” or “you know” in tone.

Strong synonym to remember: **nhưng** (more formal “but”); **mà** feels lighter and more conversational.
sentenceBreakdowns: Em (I) + muốn (want) + nước (water) + mà (but) + anh (you) + muốn (want) + trà (tea)
Anh (you) + thích (like) + cá (fish) + mà (but) + em (I) + thích (like) + gà (chicken)
Chị (you) + mua (buy) + bánh (cake) + mà (but) + mẹ (mom) + mua (buy) + sữa (milk)
exampleSentencesTranslated: I want water but you want tea.
You like fish but I like chicken.
You buy cake but mom buys milk.

example.es: Me gusta comer fruta sin azúcar.
example.vi: Tôi thích ăn trái cây mà không có đường.
example.en: I like to eat fruit without sugar.

definitions.en: a conjunction used to indicate contrast or exception
definitions.es: una conjunción utilizada para indicar contraste o excepción
definitions.vi: một liên từ được sử dụng để chỉ sự tương phản hoặc ngoại lệ

notes:
mà usually means “but” — it connects two ideas that contrast. It’s a simple, very common linker.

It joins two clauses, like English “but,” without changing word order.  

It can also soften a correction or remind someone of something, almost like “but hey” or “you know” in tone.

Strong synonym to remember: nhưng (more formal “but”); mà feels lighter and more conversational.
exampleSentences: Em muốn nước mà anh muốn trà.
Anh thích cá mà em thích gà.
Chị mua bánh mà mẹ mua sữa.
